headline: This is about building software with AI agents, not building an AI app. Same feature. Same Codex. Same acceptance test. In this verified replay, Postgres required 8,869 more estimated transcript tokens, Postgres required 70 more seconds, and Postgres required 4 more retry signals. MongoDB required 1,361 more diff bytes.
customerQuestion: When AI agents build application code, how much work is product logic and how much is database navigation?

overheadReceipt.title: Agent Overhead Receipt
overheadReceipt.headline: Same shipped workflow. Extra work charged to schema complexity.
overheadReceipt.framing: This is not a leaderboard. It is the itemized bill from one verified Codex replay pair.
overheadReceipt.outcome: Both lanes passed the same acceptance contract after starting from the same missing workflow.

items.label: Schema surface
items.publicMeaning: More objects means more context the agent must inspect, infer, and keep consistent before it can safely change product behavior.

items.label: Context spend
items.publicMeaning: The model spent more context budget getting to the same accepted workflow.

items.label: Elapsed agent work
items.publicMeaning: More agent time means slower feedback loops and more time for humans to wait, watch, or intervene.

items.label: Change footprint
items.delta: Larger MongoDB implementation footprint in this run
items.publicMeaning: This run does not claim a diff-size win for MongoDB. It keeps the unfavorable diff metric visible while the token, elapsed-time, and retry deltas remain measured.

items.label: Human review carry
items.publicMeaning: The cost model separates measured replay deltas from the human review assumption so the budget discussion stays honest.

bareMetalTrace.title: Bare Metal Agent Trace
bareMetalTrace.skepticStandard: This is a replay of captured execution, not an in-browser simulation. It proves one verified replay pair, not every workload. A new live run may differ because coding agents are nondeterministic, but the prompt, targets, local DB proof, raw traces, diffs, tests, and gates are all inspectable.

# MongoDB Customer 360 Escalation Target

This is a frozen before-state workspace for an Instrumented Agent Run.

Run:

```sh
npm test
```

The test must fail before the coding agent implements the Customer 360 Escalation Workflow. The agent should modify source code only, run the test, and leave a reviewable git diff.

# Instrumented Agent Run Rules

- Implement only the at-risk Customer 360 Escalation Workflow.
- Use the existing document-shaped collections in data/.
- Do not change the test expectations.
- Run `npm test` before finishing.
- Keep the customer-visible result identical to the acceptance contract.

agentMessages: I’ll inspect the project shape, existing workflow code, and tests first so the change stays focused on the acceptance contract.
The repo is small and clean. I’m reading the workflow, portal rendering, fixtures, and tests together to identify the narrowest missing behavior.

The target behavior is concentrated in `src/order-exception-workflow.mjs`; the projection already knows how to show persisted escalations, tasks, and customer-visible audit events. I’m going to add the workflow there, using the existing collections and policy document rather than touching fixtures or tests.

The workflow now computes the six signals from the existing collections, upserts the escalation/tasks/audit records, and leaves the portal projection path unchanged. I’m running the acceptance test next.
`npm test` passes. I’m checking the diff once so I can summarize exactly what changed and confirm no fixtures or tests moved.

Implemented the at-risk Customer 360 escalation workflow in [src/order-exception-workflow.mjs](/Users/rom.iluz/Dev/sql-hidden-cost/instrumented-agent-runs/order-exception-codex-v1/mongo/workspace/src/order-exception-workflow.mjs).

It now detects the six required risk signals from the existing document-shaped collections, persists the escalation, four owner tasks, customer-safe portal message, linked audit events, and account/order escalation state. Fixtures and tests were not changed.

Verification: `npm test` passes.

agentMessages: Inspecting the app flow and tests first so the change stays confined to the workflow surface and fixtures stay untouched.
The project is very small: one workflow module, portal rendering, one JSON table fixture, and one test file. Next I’m reading the exact acceptance assertions and existing data shape before editing.

The failing assertion is exactly the missing workflow: `applyOrderException` returns the unmodified portal view. I’m adding the escalation rows in the existing normalized arrays, deriving details from the fixture tables instead of hard-coding a separate fixture path.
The workflow now writes escalation state, risk rows, task rows, review requests, a portal message, and audit link rows. I’m running the acceptance test and will tighten any shape issues it exposes.

Implemented in [src/order-exception-workflow.mjs](/Users/rom.iluz/Dev/sql-hidden-cost/instrumented-agent-runs/order-exception-codex-v1/postgres/workspace/src/order-exception-workflow.mjs:3).

The workflow now persists the escalation, six detailed risk factors, four owner tasks, task assignments, portal message, legal/finance review requests, and customer-visible audit linkage using the existing normalized table arrays. Tests and fixture data were not changed.

Verification: `npm test` passes.

costProjection.caveat: Only the replay deltas are measured. The monthly projection changes with the assumptions selected on the page.

publicPacket.title: AI Agents Pay Your Schema Tax
publicPacket.subtitle: A public, evidence-backed replay of one ordinary application feature built against MongoDB and Postgres.
publicPacket.primaryMessage: This is not an AI app demo. It is an ordinary software change built with an AI coding agent. The agent does not just generate code; it reads your schema, infers relationships, chases state, repairs mistakes, and spends tokens doing it. This verified replay shipped the same workflow twice and captured the bill: Postgres required 8,869 more estimated transcript tokens; Postgres required 70 more seconds; Postgres required 4 more retry signals; MongoDB required 1,361 more diff bytes.

buyerPath.label: Executives
buyerPath.copy: Start with the shipped workflow and measured deltas. This is not a benchmark chart; it is a replayable application change.

buyerPath.label: Engineering
buyerPath.copy: Open trace, diff, tests, and DB proof. The claim should survive technical inspection.

buyerPath.label: Architecture
buyerPath.copy: Open the baseline credibility review. The comparison is database-native, not schema-identical and not engineered to embarrass SQL.

buyerPath.label: Finance
buyerPath.copy: Open the cost model. The projection separates measured deltas from visible assumptions.

proofRules: Same customer request and acceptance criteria.
Both workspaces start with failing acceptance.
Codex held constant across both lanes.
No hand edits after the accepted run.
Generated records are seeded into real local MongoDB and Postgres services.
The public page fails closed if verified evidence is unavailable.
caveats: This proves one Codex replay pair, not every agent or every workload.
Claude Code and Cursor require their own verified lanes before being claimed.
Cost projection depends on visible assumptions and must not be framed as guaranteed savings.
Scenario records are generated and seeded into real local services; no customer production data is used.
nextValidation: Add Claude Code with the same instrumented run contract.
Add Cursor with the same evidence gates.
Add Enterprise SQL Sprawl only after the clean Postgres baseline stays credible.
Move from local static proof lab to hosted public release with signed evidence artifacts.

claims.label: Same feature request
claims.question: Was the task identical?
claims.reason: Fairness has to be proven before any speed, token, or cost metric deserves attention.
claims.verdict: Same business task and acceptance criteria; only the database-native rule differs.

panels.label: MongoDB prompt
panels.body:
You are running an Instrumented Agent Run for MongoDB's Interactive Proof Lab.

Task:
Implement the at-risk Customer 360 Escalation Workflow for order HX-20491 / account acct-nova.

Business request:
Detect a delayed high-value order for a strategic account, combine CRM context, open support cases, invoice/payment risk, contract tier, recent product usage, shipment status, regulatory flags, and audit history. Route the account to Customer Success, Legal, Finance, and Support, show the customer-safe status in the portal, create internal owner tasks, and preserve a full audit timeline.

Acceptance:
- The customer portal title is "At-risk escalation active".
- Current status is "Executive escalation".
- Visible owner is "Customer Success + Legal + Finance + Support".
- Next step is "Executive recovery plan by 16:00".
- Risk summary is "6 signals: shipment delay, strategic account, open support case, invoice risk, usage drop, regulatory review".
- Tasks is "4 owner tasks".
- Audit history is visible.
- Customer message is "We are coordinating executive recovery for your delayed shipment."
- Persist the escalation state, all six risk factors with detail, four owner tasks with account/order/title/due context, customer portal message, and audit linkage.
- Run npm test and make it pass.

Rules:
- Do not change tests.
- Do not change generated fixture data.
- Keep the MongoDB document-shaped model native.
- Make the smallest production-style code change needed.

claims.label: Local database replay
claims.question: Did this touch real databases?
claims.reason: This proves the scenario records were seeded into real local database services and replayed there.
claims.verdict: The proof runners seed real local MongoDB and Postgres services, execute the workflow, and capture before/after workflow state.

claims.label: Baseline credibility
claims.question: Is the Postgres baseline credible?
claims.reason: This is the guardrail against dismissing the result as an intentionally weak relational baseline.
claims.verdict: The review states why both sides are database-native and why schema-identical comparison would be less fair.

panels.label: MongoDB rationale
panels.body: The MongoDB target models the account as the Customer 360 aggregate for the hot escalation path: contacts, contract, health, account team, billing summary, usage summary, support summary, compliance summary, current escalation, and task summary are co-located because the workflow reads them together. The order remains an aggregate for line items, fulfillment, payment, and bounded status history. Unbounded streams such as support cases, invoices, usage snapshots, work items, customer escalations, and audit events remain separate collections. This follows MongoDB access-pattern modeling without recreating relational table boundaries.

panels.label: Postgres rationale
panels.body: The Postgres target uses a credible normalized relational baseline with explicit tables and foreign keys for account tiers, accounts, contacts, contracts, team members, risk flags, health factors, products, orders, shipments, support cases, invoices, payment risk events, usage snapshots, compliance reviews, escalation policies, escalation cases, risk factors, owner tasks, task assignments, portal messages, legal and finance requests, status history, audit events, SLA, inventory, renewals, and notification outbox. This is a normal relational customer-360 design, not an intentionally weakened schema.

panels.label: Trade-off
panels.body: The comparison is database-native rather than schema-identical. Identical schemas would be less fair because they would punish one database for the other's natural shape. The proof therefore compares the same customer request, same fixture, same acceptance contract, and same outcome while allowing each target to use its idiomatic data model.

panels.label: Full review JSON
panels.body:
{
  "schemaVersion": "1.0.0",
  "reviewId": "customer-360-escalation-v2-rubric-review",
  "status": "captured",
  "reviewer": "Rubric-based Independent Design Review for Seller V1",
  "scenario": "Customer 360 Escalation / Strategic Account Rescue",
  "task": "At-risk Customer 360 Escalation Workflow",
  "rubric": [
    "Each database must use a database-native model.",
    "Both targets must support the same customer-visible behavior and acceptance tests.",
    "MongoDB must not be forced into table parity.",
    "Postgres must not be intentionally denormalized or weakened.",
    "Unbounded history, usage snapshots, support comments, invoices, task history, and audit trails must be modeled separately or bounded explicitly.",
    "The task must be harder because enterprise apps are harder, not because the relational target is sabotaged."
  ],
  "mongoRationale": "The MongoDB target models the account as the Customer 360 aggregate for the hot escalation path: contacts, contract, health, account team, billing summary, usage summary, support summary, compliance summary, current escalation, and task summary are co-located because the workflow reads them together. The order remains an aggregate for line items, fulfillment, payment, and bounded status history. Unbounded streams such as support cases, invoices, usage snapshots, work items, customer escalations, and audit events remain separate collections. This follows MongoDB access-pattern modeling without recreating relational table boundaries.",
  "postgresRationale": "The Postgres target uses a credible normalized relational baseline with explicit tables and foreign keys for account tiers, accounts, contacts, contracts, team members, risk flags, health factors, products, orders, shipments, support cases, invoices, payment risk events, usage snapshots, compliance reviews, escalation policies, escalation cases, risk factors, owner tasks, task assignments, portal messages, legal and finance requests, status history, audit events, SLA, inventory, renewals, and notification outbox. This is a normal relational customer-360 design, not an intentionally weakened schema.",
  "tradeoff": "The comparison is database-native rather than schema-identical. Identical schemas would be less fair because they would punish one database for the other's natural shape. The proof therefore compares the same customer request, same fixture, same acceptance contract, and same outcome while allowing each target to use its idiomatic data model.",
  "decision": "Approved for Seller V1 candidate and verified Codex replay generation using the harder Customer 360 Escalation scenario.",
  "caveat": "This is a rubric-based independent design review artifact. A later customer-facing release can replace or supplement it with named MongoDB and Postgres reviewer sign-off."
}

claims.label: Cost model
claims.question: What does the projection assume?
claims.reason: Finance needs the assumptions separated from the measured replay delta before any budget conversation is honest.
claims.verdict: The replay projects $5.09 per task and $20.4k monthly under visible assumptions, not guaranteed savings.

panels.label: Cost model JSON
panels.body:
{
  "assumptions": {
    "tokenEstimator": "ceil(captured Codex transcript bytes / 4)",
    "blendedModelCostPerMillionContextTokensUsd": 10,
    "engineeringReviewRateUsdPerHour": 150,
    "reviewMinutesPerChangedFile": 3,
    "reviewMinutesPerRetrySignal": 0.5,
    "agentTasksPerMonth": 4000
  },
  "tokenDelta": 8869,
  "reviewMinutesDelta": 2,
  "modelDeltaUsd": 0.08869,
  "reviewDeltaUsd": 5,
  "perTaskDeltaUsd": 5.08869,
  "monthlyDeltaUsd": 20354.76
}

claims.label: Verified data contract
claims.question: Can this page render unverified data?
claims.reason: Public proof must fail closed when evidence is missing, instead of quietly showing placeholder claims.
claims.verdict: The verified replay disables unverified runtime data, the console fails closed, and the evidence gate blocks retired prototype proof text.

panels.label: Runtime data contract
Verified manifest
Evidence gate
panels.body: The gate is hash-locked as a source file and executed by npm run proof:no-mock. It blocks retired prototype artifacts, unverified fallback proof, and verified replay promotion without dataContract.mockDataAllowed === false.

shareText:
MongoDB AI Build Cost Replay: verified Codex run.
Scenario: Customer 360 Escalation / Strategic Account Rescue, order HX-20491.
MongoDB local service: 41 generated records across 14 collections.
Postgres local service: 189 generated rows across 56 tables.
Measured delta in this run: Postgres required 8,869 more estimated transcript tokens; Postgres required 70 more seconds; Postgres required 4 more retry signals; MongoDB required 1,361 more diff bytes.
Scope: one verified Codex replay pair; Claude Code and Cursor require their own verified lanes before being claimed.
